---
title: "Python for Data Science"
author: "Nathasha"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
    highlight-style: github
    smooth-scroll: true
    css: stylesnew10.css
    embed-resources: true
engine: knitr
---

```{=html}
<div class="hero-banner">
  <div class="hero-content">
    <div class="hero-icon">🐍</div>
    <h1>Python for Data Science</h1>
    <p>Sections 2 Python Basics &amp; Key Libraries</p>
    <div class="hero-badges">
      <span class="badge badge-blue">Variables</span>
      <span class="badge badge-green">Operators</span>
      <span class="badge badge-orange">Control Flow</span>
      <span class="badge badge-purple">Functions</span>
      <span class="badge badge-red">Data Structures</span>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>
```

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 📋 About This Section {.unlisted .unnumbered}

This page covers **Section 2** of our Python for Data Science course — the core Python language essentials. Sections 1 (What is Python, Installation & Setup, Python Environment) is covered by our teammates. Key libraries and their deep dives are covered by other teams.
:::

## Table of Contents {.unnumbered}

- [Python Basics](#sec-basics)
  - [Variables & Data Types](#sec-variables)
  - [Operators](#sec-operators)
  - [Control Flow](#sec-control-flow)
  - [Functions](#sec-functions)
  - [Data Structures](#sec-data-structures)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Python Basics {#sec-basics}

> *"Python is an easy to learn, powerful programming language."* — Python Docs

Python is **dynamically typed**, **interpreted**, and built for readability. Here is everything we will cover in this section:

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart TD
    ROOT["🐍 Python Basics"] --> V["📦 Variables & Types<br/>int · float · str · bool · None"]
    ROOT --> O["➕ Operators<br/>Arith · Comparison · Logical"]
    ROOT --> C["🔀 Control Flow<br/>if · for · while · break"]
    ROOT --> F["⚙️ Functions<br/>def · return · lambda · scope"]
    ROOT --> D["🗂️ Data Structures<br/>List · Tuple · Dict · Set"]
    style ROOT fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff,font-weight:bold
    style V  fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff
    style O  fill:#10b981,color:#fff
    style C  fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    style F  fill:#8b5cf6,color:#fff
    style D  fill:#ef4444,color:#fff
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Variables & Data Types {#sec-variables}

::: concept-intro
In Python you never declare a type — just assign a value and Python figures out the rest. This is called **dynamic typing**.
:::

### Core Data Types

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart LR
    A["Python<br/>Data Types"] --> B["🔢 int<br/>42, -7, 0"]
    A --> C["🔡 float<br/>3.14, -0.5"]
    A --> D["📝 str<br/>'hello'"]
    A --> E["✅ bool<br/>True / False"]
    A --> F["∅ None<br/>no value"]
    style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff,font-weight:bold
    style B fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff
    style C fill:#10b981,color:#fff
    style D fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    style E fill:#8b5cf6,color:#fff
    style F fill:#6b7280,color:#fff
```

### Assigning Variables

``` python
# Assign a variable — no type declaration needed!
name       = "Alice"     # str
age        = 30          # int
height     = 5.6         # float
is_student = True        # bool
nothing    = None        # NoneType

print(name, age, height, is_student)
```

``` code-output
Alice 30 5.6 True
```

### Checking & Converting Types

``` python
print(type(name))        # <class 'str'>
print(type(age))         # <class 'int'>
print(type(height))      # <class 'float'>
print(type(is_student))  # <class 'bool'>

# Type casting
age_str    = str(age)        # int → string  →  "30"
height_int = int(height)     # float → int   →  5  (truncates!)
print(age_str, height_int)
```

``` code-output
<class 'str'>
<class 'int'>
<class 'float'>
<class 'bool'>
30 5
```

### Working with Strings

``` python
greeting = "Hello, World!"

print(greeting.upper())                      # HELLO, WORLD!
print(greeting.lower())                      # hello, world!
print(greeting.replace("World", "Python"))   # Hello, Python!
print(len(greeting))                         # 13

# f-strings — the modern, readable way to format output
language = "Python"
version  = 3.12
print(f"I am learning {language} version {version}!")
```

``` code-output
HELLO, WORLD!
hello, world!
Hello, Python!
13
I am learning Python version 3.12!
```

:::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🆚 Python vs R — Variables & Types {.unlisted .unnumbered}

::: panel-tabset
### Comparison Table

| Feature        | Python            | R                        |
|----------------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Assignment     | `x = 5`           | `x <- 5`                 |
| Check type     | `type(x)`         | `class(x)`               |
| No value       | `None`            | `NULL` or `NA`           |
| String format  | `f"Hello {name}"` | `paste0("Hello ", name)` |
| Integer suffix | Not needed        | `5L` for integer         |

### Python Code

``` python
x = 5
print(type(x))   # <class 'int'>
name = "Alice"
print(f"Hello {name}")  # Hello Alice
missing = None
```

### R Code

``` r
x <- 5
class(x)         # [1] "numeric"
name <- "Alice"
paste0("Hello ", name)  # [1] "Hello Alice"
missing <- NULL
```
:::

> **Key difference:** R uses `<-` by convention; Python always uses `=`. Python's `None` ≈ R's `NULL`. R also has `NA` for missing data — Python handles this through Pandas (`NaN`).
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Operators {#sec-operators}

### Arithmetic Operators

``` python
a = 17
b = 5

print(a + b)    # Addition
print(a - b)    # Subtraction
print(a * b)    # Multiplication
print(a / b)    # Division        → always returns float
print(a // b)   # Floor division  → integer result
print(a % b)    # Modulus         → remainder
print(a ** b)   # Exponentiation
```

``` code-output
22          ← Addition
12          ← Subtraction
85          ← Multiplication
3.4         ← Division  (always float!)
3           ← Floor division
2           ← Modulus (remainder)
1419857     ← Exponentiation
```

### Comparison & Logical Operators

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Comparison — return True or False**

``` python
x = 10
print(x == 10)  # True  — equal
print(x != 5)   # True  — not equal
print(x > 8)    # True  — greater
print(x < 8)    # False — less
print(x >= 10)  # True  — ≥
print(x <= 10)  # True  — ≤
```

``` code-output
True
True
True
False
True
True
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Logical — combine conditions**

``` python
a = True
b = False

print(a and b)  # False — both True?
print(a or b)   # True  — either True?
print(not a)    # False — opposite

# Practical example
age = 22
has_id = True
print(age >= 18 and has_id)  # True
```

``` code-output
False
True
False

True
```
:::
:::::

:::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🆚 Python vs R — Operators {.unlisted .unnumbered}

::: panel-tabset
### Comparison Table

| Operation        | Python | R                            |
|------------------|--------|------------------------------|
| Exponentiation   | `**`   | `^`                          |
| Integer division | `//`   | `%/%`                        |
| Modulus          | `%`    | `%%`                         |
| Logical AND      | `and`  | `&&` (scalar) / `&` (vector) |
| Logical OR       | `or`   | `||` (scalar) / `|` (vector) |
| Logical NOT      | `not`  | `!`                          |

### Python Code

``` python
print(2 ** 8)        # 256
print(17 // 5)       # 3
print(17 % 5)        # 2
print(True and False)# False
```

### R Code

``` r
2 ^ 8          # [1] 256
17 %/% 5       # [1] 3
17 %% 5        # [1] 2
TRUE && FALSE  # [1] FALSE
```
:::

> **Key difference:** R has two forms of AND/OR: `&&`/`||` for single values, `&`/`|` for vectors. Python's `and`/`or` only work on single values — use NumPy for vector logic.
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Control Flow {#sec-control-flow}

### `if` / `elif` / `else`

::::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}
``` python
score = 78

if score >= 90:
    grade = "A"
elif score >= 80:
    grade = "B"
elif score >= 70:
    grade = "C"
else:
    grade = "F"

print(f"Score: {score} → Grade: {grade}")
```

``` code-output
Score: 78 → Grade: C
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    S([score = 78]) --> A{score ≥ 90?}
    A -- Yes --> GA["grade = 'A'"]
    A -- No --> B{score ≥ 80?}
    B -- Yes --> GB["grade = 'B'"]
    B -- No --> C{score ≥ 70?}
    C -- Yes --> GC["grade = 'C' ✓"]
    C -- No --> GF["grade = 'F'"]
    style GC fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    style S fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff
```
:::
:::::

::: {.callout-warning icon="false"}
## ⚠️ Indentation is Mandatory! {.unlisted .unnumbered}

Python uses **spaces/indentation** to define code blocks — not curly braces. Getting indentation wrong causes an `IndentationError`.

``` python
# ✅ Correct — 4 spaces {.unlisted .unnumbered}
if True:
    print("correct")

# ❌ Wrong — no indentation {.unlisted .unnumbered}
if True:
print("error!")     # IndentationError!
```
:::

### `for` Loops

::::: columns
::: {.column width="60%"}
``` python
# Loop over a range
for i in range(1, 6):
    print(f"  {i} squared = {i**2}")
```

``` code-output
  1 squared = 1
  2 squared = 4
  3 squared = 9
  4 squared = 16
  5 squared = 25
```

``` python
# Loop over a list
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry"]
for fruit in fruits:
    print(f"  I like {fruit}")
```

``` code-output
  I like apple
  I like banana
  I like cherry
```

``` python
# enumerate() gives index + value
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(f"  [{i}] {fruit}")
```

``` code-output
  [0] apple
  [1] banana
  [2] cherry
```
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    R["range(1, 6)"] --> I{i = 1,2,3,4,5<br/>more items?}
    I -- Yes --> P["print(i squared)"]
    P --> I
    I -- No --> E([End])
    style R fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff
    style E fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
```
:::
:::::

### `while` Loops & Loop Control

:::::::::::::::::::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
``` python
count = 0
while count < 5:
    print(f"  count = {count}")
    count += 1
print("Done!")
```

``` code-output
  count = 0
  count = 1
  count = 2
  count = 3
  count = 4
Done!
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
``` python
# break — stop the loop early
for i in range(10):
    if i == 5:
        break
    print(i, end=" ")
print()

# continue — skip current iteration
for i in range(10):
    if i % 2 == 0:
        continue
    print(i, end=" ")
```

``` code-output
0 1 2 3 4 
1 3 5 7 9
```
:::

:::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🆚 Python vs R — Control Flow {.unlisted .unnumbered}

::: panel-tabset
### Comparison Table

| Feature       | Python              | R                       |
|---------------|---------------------|-------------------------|
| if block      | `if cond:` + indent | `if (cond) { }`         |
| for loop      | `for x in seq:`     | `for (x in seq) { }`    |
| while loop    | `while cond:`       | `while (cond) { }`      |
| Vectorised if | list comprehension  | `ifelse(cond, yes, no)` |
| Index start   | **0**               | **1**                   |

### Python Code

``` python
for i in range(1, 4):
    if i == 2:
        print(f"Found {i}!")
    else:
        print(i)
```

### R Code

``` r
for (i in 1:3) {
  if (i == 2) {
    cat("Found", i, "!\n")
  } else {
    print(i)
  }
}
```
:::

> **Key difference:** Python uses **4-space indentation** as syntax — not optional. R uses `{ }` braces. Also, `range(1, 6)` in Python gives `1,2,3,4,5` (stop is exclusive), while `1:6` in R gives `1,2,3,4,5,6` (stop is inclusive).
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Functions {#sec-functions}

::: concept-intro
Functions let you **reuse code**. Define once with `def`, call many times. Use `return` to send a value back.
:::

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart LR
    A["def greet(name):"] --> B["📥 Parameter<br/>name"]
    B --> C["'''docstring'''<br/>return f'Hello {name}'"]
    C --> D["📤 Return value<br/>'Hello Alice'"]
    G["greet('Alice')"] --> B
    style A fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff
    style D fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    style G fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff
```

### Basic Functions

``` python
def greet(name):
    """Return a greeting message."""
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("Alice"))
print(greet("Bob"))
```

``` code-output
Hello, Alice!
Hello, Bob!
```

### Default Arguments & `*args` / `**kwargs`

::::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
``` python
# Default argument values
def power(base, exponent=2):
    return base ** exponent

print(power(3))       # uses default
print(power(3, 3))    # override default
print(power(2, 10))
```

``` code-output
9
27
1024
```

``` python
# *args — extra positional args
def total(*args):
    return sum(args)

print(total(1, 2, 3))
print(total(10, 20, 30, 40))
```

``` code-output
6
100
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
``` python
# **kwargs — keyword arguments
def describe(**kwargs):
    for key, value in kwargs.items():
        print(f"  {key}: {value}")

describe(name="Alice",
         age=30,
         city="Colombo")
```

``` code-output
  name: Alice
  age: 30
  city: Colombo
```
:::
:::::

### Scope & Docstrings

``` python
x = "global"

def show_scope():
    x = "local"    # different variable!
    print(f"Inside: x = '{x}'")

show_scope()
print(f"Outside: x = '{x}'")
```

``` code-output
Inside: x = 'local'
Outside: x = 'global'
```

### Lambda Functions

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 💡 What is a Lambda? {.unlisted .unnumbered}

A **lambda** is a tiny, anonymous, one-line function. Use it when you need a quick function without defining a full `def` block.
:::

``` python
# Regular function vs lambda — same result
def square(x):
    return x ** 2

square_lambda = lambda x: x ** 2

print(square(5))          # 25
print(square_lambda(5))   # 25

# Lambda shines inside other functions
numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]
sorted_desc = sorted(numbers, key=lambda x: -x)
print(sorted_desc)
```

``` code-output
25
25
[9, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1]
```

:::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🆚 Python vs R — Functions {.unlisted .unnumbered}

::: panel-tabset
### Comparison Table

| Feature      | Python              | R                             |
|--------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|
| Define       | `def f(x):`         | `f <- function(x) {`          |
| Return       | Explicit `return x` | Last expression auto-returned |
| Anonymous    | `lambda x: x*2`     | `function(x) x*2`             |
| Default args | `def f(x=10):`      | `function(x=10)`              |
| Docstring    | `"""text"""`        | `#' @description` (roxygen2)  |

### Python Code

``` python
def add(a, b=10):
    """Add two numbers."""
    return a + b

print(add(5))     # 15
print(add(5, 3))  # 8
```

### R Code

``` r
add <- function(a, b = 10) {
  a + b   # auto-returned!
}

add(5)    # [1] 15
add(5, 3) # [1] 8
```
:::

> **Key difference:** In R, the **last expression** is automatically returned — no `return` keyword needed. Python always requires an explicit `return`. Both approaches are valid; Python's is more explicit.
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Data Structures {#sec-data-structures}

::: concept-intro
Python has four built-in data structures. Choosing the right one matters for both clarity and performance.
:::

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart LR
    D["Python<br/>Data Structures"] --> L["📋 List<br/>[1, 2, 3]<br/>ordered · mutable"]
    D --> T["🔒 Tuple<br/>(1, 2, 3)<br/>ordered · immutable"]
    D --> Di["📖 Dict<br/>{'a': 1}<br/>key-value pairs"]
    D --> S["🎯 Set<br/>{1, 2, 3}<br/>unique · unordered"]
    style D fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff,font-weight:bold
    style L fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff
    style T fill:#8b5cf6,color:#fff
    style Di fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    style S fill:#10b981,color:#fff
```

### Lists — Ordered & Mutable

``` python
fruits = ["apple", "banana", "cherry", "date"]

# Indexing — 0-based!
print(fruits[0])     # first element
print(fruits[-1])    # last element

# Slicing [start:stop:step]
print(fruits[1:3])   # middle slice
print(fruits[::2])   # every other

# Modify
fruits.append("elderberry")
fruits.insert(1, "avocado")
fruits.remove("banana")
print(fruits)
```

``` code-output
apple
date
['banana', 'cherry']
['apple', 'cherry']
['apple', 'avocado', 'cherry', 'date', 'elderberry']
```

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## 🔢 0-Based Indexing — The #1 Gotcha for R Users! {.unlisted .unnumbered}

```         
List:   ["apple",  "banana",  "cherry",  "date"]
Python:     [0]       [1]        [2]       [3]    ← starts at 0
R:          [1]       [2]        [3]       [4]    ← starts at 1
```

The **first element** in Python is always `[0]`. This catches every R programmer when they first learn Python!
:::

**List Comprehensions** — the Pythonic, compact way to build lists:

``` python
# [expression for item in iterable if condition]
squares      = [x**2 for x in range(1, 11)]
even_squares = [x**2 for x in range(1, 11) if x % 2 == 0]

print(squares)
print(even_squares)
```

``` code-output
[1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36, 49, 64, 81, 100]
[4, 16, 36, 64, 100]
```

### Tuples — Ordered & Immutable

``` python
# Parentheses — cannot change after creation
coordinates = (40.7128, -74.0060)   # lat, lon of New York
print(coordinates[0])

# Tuple unpacking — elegant!
lat, lon = coordinates
print(f"Latitude: {lat}, Longitude: {lon}")
```

``` code-output
40.7128
Latitude: 40.7128, Longitude: -74.006
```

### Dictionaries — Key-Value Pairs

``` python
student = {
    "name":     "Alice",
    "age":      22,
    "grades":   [88, 92, 79],
    "enrolled": True
}

# Access
print(student["name"])            # direct access
print(student.get("gpa", "N/A")) # safe access with default

# Add / update
student["university"] = "UoM"
student["age"] = 23

# Iterate
for key, value in student.items():
    print(f"  {key:12} → {value}")
```

``` code-output
Alice
N/A
  name         → Alice
  age          → 23
  grades       → [88, 92, 79]
  enrolled     → True
  university   → UoM
```

### Sets — Unique Values

``` python
# Duplicates are removed automatically
numbers = {1, 2, 2, 3, 3, 3, 4}
print(numbers)

# Set operations
a = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}
b = {4, 5, 6, 7, 8}

print("Union:       ", a | b)
print("Intersection:", a & b)
print("Difference:  ", a - b)
```

``` code-output
{1, 2, 3, 4}
Union:        {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8}
Intersection: {4, 5}
Difference:   {1, 2, 3}
```

:::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 🆚 Python vs R — Data Structures {.unlisted .unnumbered}

::: panel-tabset
### Comparison Table

| Python | R Equivalent | Notes |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| `list` | `list()` | R lists can be named; Python lists are simpler |
| `tuple` | No direct equivalent | R has no immutable sequence |
| `dict` | named `list()` | `d["key"]` in Python vs `lst$key` in R |
| `set` | `unique()` + `intersect()` | R has no set type; uses functions instead |
| `[x**2 for x in lst]` | `sapply(lst, function(x) x^2)` | List comprehension vs apply family |

### Python Code

``` python
# List
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
doubled = [x * 2 for x in nums]

# Dict
info = {"name": "Alice", "age": 30}
print(info["name"])
```

### R Code

``` r
# Vector
nums <- c(1, 2, 3, 4)
doubled <- sapply(nums, function(x) x * 2)

# Named list
info <- list(name = "Alice", age = 30)
info$name    # or info[["name"]]
```
:::
::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# A Simple Python Program: Data Structures in Action {#sec-complete-program}

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 📋 What This Program Covers {.unlisted .unnumbered}

This is a beginner-friendly program that shows how Python's four core data structures work together in a real scenario — tracking students and their course data. No external libraries needed — just pure Python.
:::

We will model a small class of students using **all four data structures** you learned:

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-align: center
flowchart LR
    A["📋 List
grades &amp; scores"] --> E["🖨️ Print
Summary"]
    B["📖 Dictionary
student records"] --> E
    C["🔒 Tuple
course info"] --> E
    D["🎯 Set
unique subjects"] --> E
    style A fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff
    style B fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    style C fill:#8b5cf6,color:#fff
    style D fill:#10b981,color:#fff
    style E fill:#1e3a5f,color:#fff
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 1 — Set Up the Data {.unlisted .unnumbered}

``` python
# ── Step 1: Use a TUPLE for fixed course information ──────────────
# Tuples are immutable — course details don't change mid-semester
course_info = ("Data Science 101", "DSC101", 2025, "Semester 1")
course_name, course_code, year, semester = course_info

print(f"Course : {course_name} ({course_code})")
print(f"Period : {semester}, {year}")
```

``` code-output
Course : Data Science 101 (DSC101)
Period : Semester 1, 2025
```

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## 💡 Why a Tuple here? {.unlisted .unnumbered}

Course details are **fixed** — they should never accidentally be changed. A tuple enforces that: `course_info[0] = "New Name"` would raise a `TypeError`.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 2 — Store Student Records {.unlisted .unnumbered}

``` python
# ── Step 2: Use a DICTIONARY for each student's record ────────────
# Dict maps student ID → their data
students = {
    "S001": {"name": "Alice",   "grades": [85, 92, 78, 90], "year": 2},
    "S002": {"name": "Bob",     "grades": [70, 65, 80, 72], "year": 1},
    "S003": {"name": "Carol",   "grades": [95, 98, 92, 97], "year": 3},
    "S004": {"name": "David",   "grades": [60, 55, 70, 65], "year": 1},
    "S005": {"name": "Eve",     "grades": [88, 84, 91, 87], "year": 2},
}

# Access a specific student
alice = students["S001"]
print(f"Student : {alice['name']}")
print(f"Grades  : {alice['grades']}")
print(f"Year    : {alice['year']}")
```

``` code-output
Student : Alice
Grades  : [85, 92, 78, 90]
Year    : 2
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 3 — Analyse Grades with Lists {.unlisted .unnumbered}

``` python
# ── Step 3: Use a LIST to calculate each student's average ─────────
# Lists are perfect for ordered collections we want to process

print(f"{'Name':<10} {'Grades':<25} {'Average':>8} {'Result':>8}")
print("-" * 55)

results = []   # we'll build this list as we go

for sid, info in students.items():
    grades  = info["grades"]           # this is a list
    average = sum(grades) / len(grades)
    result  = "PASS" if average >= 65 else "FAIL"

    results.append({
        "name":    info["name"],
        "average": average,
        "result":  result
    })

    print(f"{info['name']:<10} {str(grades):<25} {average:>8.1f} {result:>8}")
```

``` code-output
Name       Grades                    Average   Result
-------------------------------------------------------
Alice      [85, 92, 78, 90]             86.2     PASS
Bob        [70, 65, 80, 72]             71.8     PASS
Carol      [95, 98, 92, 97]             95.5     PASS
David      [60, 55, 70, 65]             62.5     FAIL
Eve        [88, 84, 91, 87]             87.5     PASS
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 4 — Find Unique Subjects with a Set {.unlisted .unnumbered}

``` python
# ── Step 4: Use a SET to track unique subjects enrolled ────────────
# Sets automatically remove duplicates

enrolments = [
    "Maths", "Python", "Statistics",
    "Python", "Maths", "Data Viz",
    "Statistics", "Python", "Machine Learning",
    "Maths", "Data Viz"
]

unique_subjects = set(enrolments)

print(f"Total enrolments logged : {len(enrolments)}")
print(f"Unique subjects offered : {len(unique_subjects)}")
print(f"Subjects: {sorted(unique_subjects)}")

# Set operations — which subjects are core vs elective?
core_subjects     = {"Maths", "Python", "Statistics"}
elective_subjects = unique_subjects - core_subjects

print(f"\nCore subjects    : {sorted(core_subjects)}")
print(f"Elective subjects: {sorted(elective_subjects)}")
```

``` code-output
Total enrolments logged : 11
Unique subjects offered : 5
Subjects: ['Data Viz', 'Machine Learning', 'Maths', 'Python', 'Statistics']

Core subjects    : ['Maths', 'Python', 'Statistics']
Elective subjects: ['Data Viz', 'Machine Learning']
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Step 5 — Print the Final Summary {.unlisted .unnumbered}

``` python
# ── Step 5: Combine everything into a final summary ─────────────
print("=" * 50)
print(f"  COURSE SUMMARY — {course_name}")
print("=" * 50)

# Count passes and fails from our results list
passes = [r for r in results if r["result"] == "PASS"]
fails  = [r for r in results if r["result"] == "FAIL"]

# Best student — find dict with highest average
best = max(results, key=lambda r: r["average"])

print(f"  Students enrolled : {len(students)}")
print(f"  Passed            : {len(passes)}")
print(f"  Failed            : {len(fails)}")
print(f"  Top student       : {best['name']} ({best['average']:.1f}%)")
print(f"  Subjects available: {len(unique_subjects)}")
print(f"  Course period     : {semester}, {year}")
print("=" * 50)
```

``` code-output
==================================================
  COURSE SUMMARY — Data Science 101
==================================================
  Students enrolled : 5
  Passed            : 4
  Failed            : 1
  Top student       : Carol (95.5%)
  Subjects available: 5
  Course period     : Semester 1, 2025
==================================================
```

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-note icon="false"}
## 📋 What Each Step Used {.unlisted .unnumbered}

| Step | Data Structure | Why This One? |
|------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|
| **1 — Course info** | `tuple` | Fixed data that must not change |
| **2 — Student records** | `dict` | Look up any student instantly by ID |
| **3 — Grade analysis** | `list` | Ordered scores we loop over and calculate |
| **4 — Unique subjects** | `set` | Automatically removes duplicate enrolments |
| **5 — Summary** | All four | Combine everything — this is real Python! |

Every concept is pure Python — **no libraries needed**. This is the kind of small program you will write constantly as you learn data science.
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Quick Reference {#sec-reference}

## Python Basics at a Glance

| Concept         | Python                | R Equivalent          |
|-----------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|
| Assign variable | `x = 5`               | `x <- 5`              |
| Check type      | `type(x)`             | `class(x)`            |
| List            | `[1, 2, 3]`           | `c(1, 2, 3)`          |
| Dict            | `{"a": 1}`            | `list(a=1)`           |
| if block        | `if x > 0:` + indent  | `if (x > 0) { }`      |
| for loop        | `for i in range(10):` | `for (i in 1:10) { }` |
| Define function | `def f(x): return x`  | `f <- function(x) x`  |
| Anonymous fn    | `lambda x: x * 2`     | `function(x) x * 2`   |
| First index     | `lst[0]`              | `lst[1]`              |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: {.callout-important icon="false"}
## 🚀 Next Steps — What Other Teams Cover {.unlisted .unnumbered}

| Topic                                        | Who covers it        |
|----------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| NumPy — arrays, broadcasting, linear algebra | NumPy team           |
| Pandas — data wrangling, time-series         | Pandas team          |
| Matplotlib — plots, themes, visualisations   | Matplotlib team      |
| statsmodels — regression, survival analysis  | Regression team      |
| Modules, pip & the import system             | Libraries intro team |

This page is your **Python language foundation** — bookmark it and return to it as you work through the library-specific sections!
:::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

*Content based on the Python Data Science Handbook by Jake VanderPlas (O'Reilly, 2017). Examples adapted for teaching.*
::::::::::::::::::::::
